CURRENT AFFAIRS.

““ The Rise of Matty Bodkin ** is still a topic of much
conversation. Why does not the Zreeman let us know
what it thinks of the place that its ex-leader writer has
Leen shovelled into? A letter from Mr. A. M. Sul-
livan, B.L., is printed in the - I"remfan of
Monday on the question of posts in the
gift of the Government. - Mr. Sullivan em-
phasises the point that what Catholics and Nationalists
want in the matter of the position of power and emolu-
ment is fair play only. As he says:—* For those who

 desire that a certain percentage of the wages of political
and religious prostitution should be set aside for Papists

there is no need of agitation. The present system pro-
vides for that. Its barriers against our race and creed
have proved the truth of Shiels’ prophecy that over
them a slug might crawl though a lion could not leap-.
Trishmen and Catholics who are willing to trade on their
professions of National and religious convictions can no
doubt get some bargains in the bribery market.”

Again he remarks: —‘‘ Our National demand is that
a career shall be open for all talent in Ireland and that
public patronage shall be honestly exercised in favour
of merit, and shall cease to be sold for the dishonourable
prices for which it is now dispensed. So long as bribery
by the Castle is permitted so long must this country be
ground down by ascendancy. Corruption is a most
necessary weapon for tyranny, and only in honesty can
a democracy find freedom. I have no objection to the
appointment of Unionists or Protestants to positions of
trust or emolument in Ireland. If the best qualified
man for a vacant position is a Protestant Unionist he
certainly should be appointed‘and his appointment can-
niot fail to be an act of justice and of benefit to the
Catholic community, just as under the present system
the appointment of an incompetent and unqualified
fellow, because he professes to be a Catholic and a
Nationalist, is a gross act of injustice to every Irish
Catholic in the country.””

In another column ‘‘ Chanel’’ discusses the ever burn-
ing question of ‘“jobs.” We certainly hesitate to
agree that the Matty Bodkin incident is a line-ball case.
Matty was a militant politician in his capacity as leader-
writer on the Daily Dwindler. Our own views on the
whole situation that incidentally involves the question
of ““jobs”’ are well known. ~We have held, and
vigorously held at that, that Nationalists should fight
for all the power that they could win by fight in this
country. We think that the Irish Parliamentary Party
should use all their influence as a party in demanding
that men in Ireland should not be excluded from posi-
tions of power and emolument because of their politics
or religious profession. The whole thing should be done
openly and above board. We think it right
and proper that members of the Party should bind
themselves not to take office of any kind, nor
to ask for posts for any individuals; but the matter of
fighting for justice, tolerance and fair play vigorously,
openly, and as a party, is a different matter; it should
be one of the aims of the Party and of the Party’s poli-
tical organisation to dispute every appointment of
pewer and profit in the Nationalist interest where it
could be done consistently with justice and fair play.
Active politicians, and a prominent leader-writer on
the Freeman must be included in that term, have no
business in looking for or taking judgeships or other
posts in the patronage of the Government. The Free-
man’s Matty has sneaked into a job, and let the Freeman
explain its position; if it remains silent the Matty
Bodkin job will tend further to dwindle its dwindling
dividends. How does anyone know that Mr. Brayden,
B.L., may not be looking for something as well as
Matty? We have no information on the matter; we
Laye not heard even a rumour, but the Matty business
naturally tends to put the Freeman under an
additional cloud. Anyway, it was a great thing for
us that we were hated and, boycotted by the daily
print, one of whose leader-writer’s is now a County
Court Judge! It is better and more significant than
a thousand illuminated addresses and testimonials.
Matty of the Freeman did not love us, no more than
does the Independent.

Will anything come out of all the Commissions that
have been and are being held concerning various phases
of work and life in this country? We have ceased to
be interested in the Railway Commission; a twenty-
four-hours’ day—even if nature did not demand that
part of it should be given to sleep—is too short for all
these Commissions. In Monday’s ‘‘ Freeman’’ there
were ten and a half columns of the Railway Viceregal
Commission! When did this Railway Commission com-
mence its sitting? So far as our unaided amemory goes,
its birth is almost lost in the mists of antiquity. The
Commission is sitting in London now, and we see that
the Chairman announced that they had decided that the
Railway Companies must be prepared to commence their
case abt the beginning of Aprill This is only
November—then  we have December,  January,
February, March, April, and at the bc"inniué
of the last-named month the Railway Compzmbics are
to commence their case. Well, if they are as quick as
some of their train services we may expect the Com-
missiog to be over sometime in the ye}u' 1909. Oh,
Commissions, where are your reports? They are gone
in the wind! By dragging out these Commissions to
a sufficiently dreary length, the whole country will be
sick of even their headings in the newspapers before
they are finished. Then the Government can issue its
Blue Book which practically no one will read, and it
can safely keep on doing nothing.

We recently drew attention to a Hungarian Lottery
affair, touting particulars of which we received at
our private address. This Hungarian Lottery or
““ policy >’ for green people emanated from a Messrs.
Thule and Co., 14 City Hall Street, Budapesth. A few
days ago an eminent Irishman sent us up yet another
touting Hungarian Lottery Circular which came into
his hands. Our distinguished corespondent adds:
“ Perhaps you might direct Edward VIII.’s attention
to it !

The touting circular sent us by our correspondent
emanates from ‘‘ Bank, A. Térok and Co., Ltd.”” We
don’t mind giving this particular = ‘‘ Hungarian
policy,”” for lifting shillings from the green people of
this country, an advertisement, as we observe that  we
cannot guarantee same to be duly executed only when
we receive your remittance not later than November
21st, 1907, the day of the beginning of the drawing.’”
Apparently the Hungarians who have to do with lotteries
think that green Ireland is a verdant field for their
operations. However, we doubt, green _though the
grass grows over here, if the people are quite as green
as these Hungarians may think. Needless to say, we
advise our readers to have nothing whatever to do with
these Hungarian enterprises.

We are glad to learn that a library is now in course of
ation in the Rathmines and Rathgar Catholic Asso-
ciation. Father O’Donnell, of Rathmines, has kindly
provided a case and fine collection of books, and another
generous contribution of literature has been made by

The Christmas present is a time-honoured institution,
and the season for it is now approaching. We hope
that all Irish-Trelanders giving presents will not let the
opportunity pass of supporting Irish manufacture
wherever possible. In this connection we have received
a catalogue from Mr. D. V. Stephens, Ballyshannon,
which should be of interest to any of our readers who
may wish to delight their friends at Christmas with a
present of the china or earthenware variety. The cata-
logue, which is profusely illustrated, furnishes an exten-
sive list of articles in this line manufactured at the well-
known Belleek Pottery Works, with full particulars of
prices, dimensions, ete.

The ““enclosed ”” refers to a neatly turned out pro-
spectus, entitled ‘ A Greater St. Louis,’” of a St. Louis
Insurance Co. It is an appeal to *“ My. St. Louis Man’’
to keep his insurance business in St. Louis and not send
it to any other State, or States, of the Union. We
quote from the prospectus: —¢‘ You want St. Louis' to
grow, you want St. Louis’ industries to grow. You
profess pride in their growth. Such being the case, wo
now call upon you to assist us in keeping’ at Home the
ten millions of dollars drained from the State of Mis-
souri. alone, in Life Insurance premiums cach year, sent
East to make a greater Rockefeller, a greater Morgan,
and a greater Harriman--when you might have been
making a greater St. Louis. Can’t these millions be
used in St. Louis? Won’t they Hhelp you to make
a ¢ Greater St. Louis?’ ”’

And again: —‘‘ Its investmonts will be made in The
West, nott Wall Street. It is helping to make a Greater
St. Louis. We are first to meet the demands of the
people, and we offer Greater St. Louis Life Insurance
for greater St. Louis people.”

Here we have the go-ahead Yank appealing to St.
Louis patriotism as against the claims of New York.
In this country we practically have no Irish Insurance
Company now—certainly not one of any magnitude.
Practically all’ our insurance is done for us by English,
Scotch, ¢ 1d° Americans. Well, we opened’ our columns
to the discussion' of the Irish- Insurance problem at
great length, we even placed our offices at the disposal
of several gentlemen interested’ in the matter, but
nothing practical came of it. So far as insurance matters
go we remain a very emerald gem in the western world.

Ireland is justly famous for her good products, and' so
there is nothing to wonder at that Edwards’ Desiccated
Soups have a big name. Industrial Revivalists will be
interested to learn that the manufacture of these soups
is the mainspring of an Irish industry employing over
300 hands, and, in addition to the big wages bill which
this figure indicates, - the manufacturers, Messrs.
Frederick King & Co., Ltd., Belfast, are responsible for
keeping many thousands of pounds in the country by
the purchase of Irish materials for their manufacture.

There was a rather curious case tried at the Tralee
Quarter Sessions. The administrator of the estate of
the late T. C. Goodman sued Dr. John R. Hayes, of
Tralee, for £60. According to the evidence Dr. Hayes
was a tenant on the estate of Mr. Bateman, at Ballin-
orig, for which the late Mr. Goodman was agent.
There was a meeting of the tenants to consider the ques-
tion of purchasing, and they failed to agree.  Dr.
Hayes deposed that the late Mr. Goodman came to him
and said that the landlord had offered him three per
cent. on the sale, and he asked Dr. Iayes to use his
influence to induce the tenants to buy and he would
give him a commission. Dr. Hayes endeavoured to in-
duce the tenants to buy, and after several meetings the
sale of the estate was arranged. Dr. Hayes got £60
for his work in the matter. It does not come out in
evidence whether the tenants knew that Dr. Hayes
was working for a commission or working as a public-
spirited man, anxious that the tenants should buy out
their holdings. Dr. IMayes deposed that he was not
satisfied with the terms on which he himself bought;
we wonder are the other tenants satisfied? Dr. Hayes,
according to a report in a local paper, deposed: ‘‘ Mr.
Bateman refused to sell unless all the tenants bought
and were unanimous about it. I went on several
occasions round to the tenants, and several mecetings
were called to try and get them agree to purchase.
That was how I earned this money, and I thought I was
entitled to it, as promised.” Now, did the tenants
whom Dr. Hayes, of Tralee, induced to purchase know
that Dr. Flayes was engaged in' a business deal for
which he was to get £60 if he carried it off? We are
not clear on that point. Of course, if the tenants knew
that Dr. Hlayes was exerting himself to: effect a sale in
order that he might earn £60, well ‘and good. We

which Dr. Hayes acted. As it stands the report in the

haps one of the tenants who bought wo_uld clear up the

Court Judge Shaw to stick to the £60.

Tere is an extract: — "Twan’t till T was in the thrain
for about two hours, Misther O’Flaherty, that I began
to wandher how big Ireland was. Talk about maps an’
continents, but I think if Ireland got her proper rights,
an’ was properly measured, she’d be found as big as
c’er a continent iv ’em, so she would, only they’re
swindlin’ us in everything, sir, that’s what they're
doin’.””  Of course, the ‘‘Irishman ’’ that talks like
this is a Papist, for we read: —‘“ An’ when we stopped
outside the Exhibition I thought ’twas the Last Day
was in it, so I did, an’ I blessed meself hefore I thried

going th” other way.” An’ sure enough I see his fishin’
rod turned to the back, but I didn’t know the manin’
‘iv it at the time.”” This is ha’penny humour indeed!

At the last meeting of Coiste an Bhros-
tuighte (Propagandist Committee) of the Gaelic
League it was decided to divide its activities inte
sections. Miss Hayden, M.A., was allotted charge of
the leaflets and pamphlets, and Mr. Dermot Trench,
who had been in quest of speakers for propagandist
meetings, reported that he had already received promises
to deliver Gaelic League addresses when required from
the following Gaelic Leaguers:—The Hon. William Gib-
son, Dr. S. P. Mac Enri, Dr. Lynch, Father Matt Ryan,
Maire ni Aodain, M.A.; Eamonn Ceannt, Rev. F. FL.
O’Connell, Michael Ua Cleirig, Brian Ua hUigin,
Loghan Ua Neachtain, “ Feargus Finn Bheil,” Dr.
Coun Ua Murchada, and Sean Mac an Bhaird. The
Committee decided to invite applications from non-
Gaelic League Literary Societies for speakers, and it is
hoped by this means to bring Gaelic League ideas before
the intelligent public of the country. The above
speakers are prepared to deliver addresses on the work
and ideals of the Gaelic League under its various as-
pects, and it is hoped that literary societies and other
bodies will avail themselves of the opportunity to in-
vestigate the merits of the Gaelic movement by the aid of
members of the organisation who are competent to ex-
pound its aims and meaning. It may not, of course, be
always possible for the Committee to provide a speaker
in response to each individual application. Difficulties
as regards date and distance of the locality where help
is needed from the residence of the nearest speaker may
sometimes prove insuperable. However, the Committee
trust that in the majority of cases they will be able to
comply with requests for speakers.  Societies desiring
to move in the matter should apply to Diarmuid Tren.
seach, Hon. Sec., Propagandist Committee, 30 Hatch
Street, Dublin.

the mere ‘ Commercial Theatre.”” = But the author of
** Cathleen ni Houlihan ” has been to a ‘‘ God save the
King *’ dinner. At one time the ‘‘Commercial Theatre’”

theatre that was once a morgue, and at another time

ence of the British tripper to Killarney. However,

expect?! Mr. Harvey is brought to patronise and he

Hop bitters has become of late years one of the most
popular temperance beverages in this country. Up to a
few years ago we understand all our needs in this line
were imported, but a change has come over this as well
as other branches of industry, and at present there are
several very successful manufacturers of hop bitters in
Ireland. Amongst the first to take a lively interest in
this department of production were Messrs. E. Smith-
wick & Sons, Ltd., St. Francis Abbey Brewery, Kil-
kenny. At first they merely brewed to meet the neces-
sities of their own bottlings, but the high quality of
their manufacture eventually created a brisk outside

.demand which has increased their oufput to a very
qualifying extent during the past three or four years.
They are breaking new ground every year and now send
to places so far distant as Cork, Dublin, Limerick, and
Wexford, ete., which makes it all the more surprising to
us to learn that some traders who are great at passing
patriotic resolutions cannot see their way to support the
local industry. We do not decry the utility of senti-
ment, but in Irish business matters the question of

The report of the serious illness of Dr. Douglas Hyde *
has created widespread sorrow, a sorrow that is by_ no {
means confined within the expansive limits of the Irish-
Ireland world. The news of his severe illness came as
a shock to the public generally, as well as to Gaelic
Leaguers all over the country. Dr. Hyde is unique in
that he is the only possible man for the responsible posi-
tion of President of the Gaelic League—for that posi-
tion he has no rivals; and, apart from the personal feel-
ing of kindliness which is felt for him wherever he is
known, the country has reason to be anxious that for
the good of the Gaelic League he may be spared long
years of vigorous life to act as its President.

THE GAELIC LEAGUE IN ENGLISH-SPEAKING
DISTRICTS. .

OMETIMES it is easier to write a book, not to say
an article, than to find a name for it. I wrote
the name of this paper first and found that part very
easy. But when I tackled the subject I discovered that
the name didn’t exactly fit—but it was too late tochange
it—for it is only incidentally that I have to deal with
the Gaelic League in the English-speaking districts—
what I am particularly concerned with are the people
who are outside, and are likely for the most part te
remain outside the active ranks of the Gaelic League
in the big towns and cities.

Before writing this paper I looked over an article
I wrote on the Gaelic League about eight years ago in
the NVew Ireland Review. Too often, perhaps, if one
looks at an article on affairs that is eight years old, his
earnest hope is that none of his opponents or critics
may dig it up and confront him with it. Happily I was
filled with no such hope when I re-read my own article.
I put a few things in it that are fresh and true, I think,
in the present situation. One of them was—

If it be desirable that people should be drawn out they must
be given room for what is petty in them as well as for what is great.
If the Gaelic Revival is to rise on a sound foundation it must give
everyone his head, and let him do the best he can with it, be 1t
much or little—in most cases, no doubt, it will be little. If it
should descend to be a machine for manufacturing formule to be
swallowed without mastication, I can already see its limits,

It is a big job to lift a nation an inch or two; it is
comparatively easy to change and alter it; the lifting is
a different matter. And the objective of the Irish Re-
vival is the lifting up of Ireland, and, more than that,
lifting her up Irish. After ten or more years of the
Irish Revival we have changed the course of many
forces, but I am not satisfied at the extent to which
the country has been lifted. In the article I have re-
ferred to I pointed out that when we spoke of the
English-speaking people of Ireland, we spoke of an un-
known, or rather unanalysed, quantity; they have been
partly analysed since then, but yet they remain very
much unknown still. ;

What are theso people really capable of?  What are their limits
and possibilities? The riddle is rendered more complex by the
fact that they are all in a state of general affectation, playing
up to a civilization that is not natural to them. What they would
do should it ocour to them that they ought to be men and women,
and strike out originally for themselves, must remain for some
time at least a matter of hazy speculation. In fact whether there
is or is not any chance of them endeavouring to be men and women
is difficult to determine. All we can be sure of is that the Gaelic
League has, in them, a motley gathering to work upon. Observe.
it in the music halls yelling inanely at low jokes and indecent
songs; watch it coming from a patriotic meeting roaring ““The
Boys of Wexford” between the ‘‘half-way” houses; see it in
petticoats in its thousands filing into the circulating libraries and .
tho penny novelotte shops for reams of twaddle about Guy and
Belinda; listen to. it in the literary clubs discussing, as the foot-
man might discuss his master in the security of the kitchen, the
ideas of English literary men, and never for a moment becoming
conscious that God also gave it ‘a head which He intended it
to use in some original and independent manner. Watch the
motley throng wherever you go, the eyes of its members turned.

mork a fundamental change in all that.

From the Irish point of view we‘have in‘the English-
speaking centres to deal with a mind diseased and a
body enfeebled. It is a case calling for a long and
slow cure, and not for an all-of-a-sudden remedy. You
eannot do much in the matter of :affairs in Ircland, or
probably in any other country, unless you look at things
with «what I may call an objective eye; and the in-
dividual mind in Ireland—in patriotic Ireland anyway
—is inclined to be unduly subjective. ~ We all know
from our experience that the vast majority are not very
patriotic—if they are, they are too modest to show it.
Any plan for spreading the Irish language or anything
else based on the assumption that the present Anglicised
TIrish generation is positively patriotic is, it seems to me,
bound to fail ; and yet the assumption largely holds that
we are such a people. We may acquire the habit of
patriotism in time; I hope we will. We must not lose
sight of the great significance of the fact that we are
a ‘people whose national language :is English now; and
we who expect, and rightly expect, a lot.of big con-
sequences to follow when we are Irish-speaking again,
should be very much alive to the fact that a lot of con-
sequences must have already followed ‘from the fact of
our becoming an English-speaking race. The problem
in-what are called the Irish-speaking districts, which, as
you know, is a name loosely given to many places in the
country where the people can ‘talk Irish but don’t, is
rather distinct from the problem in the districts where
all speak English and can speak mno other language,
although they may have passed in French, Greek, and
Latin in the Intermediate. Father Dinneen and others
look after the former problem. The rural English-
speaking districts are a special problem :of their own
swhich Father Matt Ryan, of Knockavilla, and Father
Matt Maguire, of Trillick, in Tyrone, have shown those
who may require to know how to ‘tackle. The
relations, or perhaps want of them, between the Gaelic
League and Gaelic Leaguers, and the vast majority of
the people in the big towns and cities—those people who
are outside the League and uninterested in the Irish
language movement—present another problem that :at
this stage of the development in the movement may be
considered.

I believe that what T may call the intensive-culture
within the boundaries of Gaelic Leaguedom is very con-
siderable; this year there was an Irish-spaking dno
Féir.  But when you take an objective view of Ireland,
particularly urban and what is called ‘“highly respect-
able ”’ Ireland, this intensive culture, though valuable,
is,only a small thing. We have gone.out to the fight to
win, and after all our fighting there is a lot of winning
to be done yet. Even a weekly class followed by a
dance in a district of 20,000 or 30,000 ;people-would not
inspire a patriotic brass band to.come down outside the
door and play ‘“See the Conquering Hero -Comes.””
The commercial and professional classes are in the
main still untouched; go ito Punchestown Races, to a
tegatta, to the Horse Show, to the theatres in Dublin,
bg the theatre in Cork, and where is Irish-Ireland?
And winning ‘the fight that we are engaget in involves
converting all that to the Trish-Treland point of view.

The circumstances and the objectives of five years ago

They care ncw; then the very sound of Irish moved

THE “JOB” QUESTION.

HE appointment of Judge Bodkin has.set us all
thinking—on the great subject of jobs, and two
reflections apparently inconsistent have, I believe, oc-
curred to most minds—(1) That there is something un-
pleasant in the coming into existence of Jut'lgc Bodkin.
That this feeling is sufficiently real to many is proved by
the single fact that the ZFreeman has mot up to the
moment of my writing commented on his appomtmr':nh
(2) Secondly, a sentiment prima facie opposite has arisen
often in the same bosoms, namely, ithat there ave mot
enough Judge Bodkins on the bench. Mr. George
McSweeny—I thought he must surely have picked up a
copy of the LeaDER in the train coming back f{cm'l ses-
sicns—furnished .a very interesting .body .of statistics in
the papers the other morning, showing how the exclusion
of Catholics from office is, if anything, somewhat worse
than was generally believed, and is being rigidly adhered
to under the present Government.

Now, what makes Judge Bodkin’s appointment so
interesting is that he is, as it were, a line-ball case. He
had long ceased to be a member of Parliament, he was
not taking any public part in politics, but he was up to
the.day of his promotion .writing leaders inthe Freeman.
And it can hardly be doubted that, while hoping for the
present appointment, his action as a leader-writer must
necessarily have been influenced by his hopes of prefer-
ment. Ie would have been more than human if any
time these six months, when through force of habit he
had written ‘“ the infamies of the Government,’” he had
not crossed out ‘‘infamies” and substituted ‘¢ indis-
cretions.”” For it is axiomatic that whoever amongst
us gets a job, gets it by subserviency greater or less,
that is, if he does not get it by treachery. .On the other
hand, does anyone really desire that no persons of
Nationalist leanings should ‘be in the public service, that;
every law-case, civil or criminal, for instance, in which
a Nationalist is concerned, should be tried by
a judical personage with animal passions inflamed
against him.  When in Parnell’s time Home Rule
seemed, and very nearly was, a matter of a year or two,
such an attitude had its justification, but now-a-days
Mr. ‘Redmend speaks of the next generation.

Now, there are three kinds of jobs, and likewise three
kinds of job-getters. You have (1) Jobs obtained by
competitive examination ; (2) Neutral positions, such as
educational ones, or that the late Dr. Coyne had, and
(8) ‘Political governing jobs. And, as for job-getters,
you ‘have (1) Examination competitors; (2) Neutral
personages, who mind their own ‘business; (3) Political
personages. Now, as to the.combination of the third
in each, or even the third with the second, we are all
agreed that if a.member of iParliament, were to take a
job and a fortiori.a governing political job, if Mr. ‘Red-
mond or Mr. Harrington, for instance, were to do so
(not suggesting that they have any such intention), it
would be :to repeat «the ‘infamy of Judge Keogh. At
the other end of the scale, it is commonly enough thrown
in:the teeth of civil servants, who express violent politi-
cal .opinions—they naturally :hate the .Government, as
tho servant hates her mistress—that:they are in the pay
of the I.B.G. But this is rather a useful taunt . than

. true sentiment, for.even persons.of the strongest. poli-

tical views seem :fo ‘have no objection to .entering .or
sending their children into the Civil :Service by .com-
petitive, examination.

The most iinteresting question is that of more or less
neutral -men, taking (2) mneutral or (3) :Govern-
ment positions. Undoubtedly they -must :be at
the -best complacent, both :before and after takin
them, and equally undoubtedly;the bloom of their placigl
nationalism gradually wears off. Yet, they ave, with a
few, disgraceful; exceptions, much betterthan me’u taken
from the other side, and few will deny that it would
be far better-to have a reasonable share of;such..men
who, at any rate, start.nationalist, in. public ,pasitions.
We should all have been gratified, for instance, to se(;
the ‘late ‘Mr. Michael Dunn, K.C., a County, Court,
g;dgc. I.do not know anything about the politics of

Mr. Bellingham Brady, Catholic Assistant Commis-
“sioner of Intermediate Education, but when his place

‘becomes vacant, should we desire to see him succeeded
by a Unionist, a bitter opponent of the Irish lan-
guage, &c.?

YB.ub it is in regard to getting the jobs for the
}\ahou?,hst neutrals that the real questiou arises who is
to get jobs for them? Is the Nationalist Party to ask
fcrlthem? At present they are under a self-denying
ordinance not to do so. Like most such ordinances of
the sort T am inclined to conjecture that it works some-
what unevenly. Else, how did Messrs. A. B. and C get
their positions? Yet it is a salutary principle, if con-
sistently adhered to, that Irish politicians should not
ask‘ for jobs for individuals, or allow their political
action to be influenced by any considerations of jobbery.
It can be defended even on the lowest and most mer-
cenary grounds.  What number of jobs got by asking
would have compensated for any postponement of the
Local Government Act? And similar arguments might
be used in regard to the Council Bill. Jobs may be

to protest against the exclusion of neutral Nationalists

which they have entirely neglected, to demand that a
far larger number of positions—Resident Magistracies,
to take but one example—be thrown open to competi-
tive examination in which Catholics always hold their
own. This is a form of sympathetic government not
hindered by the House of Lords, and each advance in
this direction is a permanent gain.

I have made these suggestions because it is necessary
to have some definite attitude in a matter which is the
subject of so much expectancy and so many recrimina-

TRINITY COLLEGE AGRICULTURE.

RINITY College has done some queer things !ately.
Although she has become a stale, old max}i, we
now find her donning the robes of Ceres, and trying to
look benignly on Ireland, same as that goddess,-seated
upon the Roman Capital, smiled on the known world.
A little over a year ago, Trinity added a course of scien-
tific agriculture to her curriculum. She founded a Chair
of Agriculture, and installed therein Mr. W. A. Barnes,
of Westland, Moynalty, Co. Meath. The questions
arise—(1) Why this new-born zeal for scientific agricul-
ture on the part of Trinity College? and (2) is the Col-
lege a place suited to farm education? Probably, Pro-
fessor Barnes had these awkward questions in mind
when he was delivering his inaugural address in the
Engineering School of Trinity College, on the 8th inst.
The Provost presided on the interesting occasion, and
introduced Professor Barnes as a ‘‘ skilled agriculturist,””
and ‘‘ one of the greatest land valuers in Ireland.’”’ Pro-
fessor Barnes is the erstwhile celebrated land valuer,
whose powers in swearing on behalf of the landlords is
well, but certainly not favourably, known in the Land
Court records. Provost Traill, as a landlord, naturally
regards Professor Barnes’ valuations as ‘‘ great;”’ why
did not the Provost use the more appropriate expres-
sion, ‘‘ prodigious,”’ like the ‘‘Dominie’’ in Scott’s
novel ?

After giving expression to a number of platitudes,
‘“the Professor’’ delivered himself of the following
astounding piece of ironical oratory:—‘‘ He tendered,
on behalf of the farmers of Ireland, their best thanks
to one who had 'done so much by his energy and persever-
ance to start the School of Agriculture there, and who,
moreover, had, with great liberality, founded a prize in
connection with that School, which would for the future
bear his name. He trusted that others would follow the
example so nobly set up by Mr. John H. Edge’’
(applause). ~ What is Professor Barnes thanking Mr.
Edge for on behalf of the Irish farmers? Certainly not
for the ‘‘fair’’ (?) rents fixed by the notorious Edge
Sub-Land Commission Court. These laudations of Mr.
Edge, by Professor Barnes, are ‘‘ great value,”’ from a
comic point of view, but Mr. Barnes’ ‘‘ great valua-
tions *’ in the Land Court are something serious between
God and man. Continuing his address, Professor Barnes
said :—‘“ He wished to bear witness to the great
amount of good which had been effected by the Depart-
ment of Agriculture throughout the country. . . .
Nothing could be more devoid of foundation than that
the School of Agriculture in Trinity College had been
started more or less in opposition to, and in competition
with, the institutions of thé Agricultural Department
(hear, hear). The sole wish of that School was not to
compete with, but to co-operate with, the Department
in spreading light throughout Ireland, and there was
scope enough for both of them in that respect.”’ Here
we have a' tangible explanation of the School—an
apologia pro vita sua.

There are a number of valuable offices at disposal of
the Department, as well as under the Land Commission,
the Estates Commissioners, etc., and Trinity wants to be
in the field, with her alumni, in an endeavour to cap-
ture everything good that is going for *‘ the right sort.”””
The whole thing seems a very knacky piece of business.
It means the coaching and qualifying for the above
appointments, a lot of vain, self-seeking, so-called scien-
tific agriculturists, creatures who will be afraid to g0
outside their shell, the crust of which has been so in-
durated by long continued incubation of the old hatch-
ing hen of ascendancy, that the poor chicken must needs
keep pecking for a long time before it can peep out into
the world of Irish enlightened ideas, in respect of agricul-
tural and industrial progress. The Dons of Trinity are.

first-rate incubators, and they keep hatching for all the
Government appointments, so as to bring out their own
offspring as heirs in continuity to their very unjustly-
acquired and wrongfully retained acquisitions.

No art—for it is an art—has been so grossly mis-

The faults to be met with everywhere in TIrish litera-

not always to the want of ability on the part of the

At the preseny day the most serious attempts at criti-

It is a most deplorable feature in Irish controversy

the Aristotelian method, which exercised a most bene-
ticial influence at one time, bbut has now fallen into
disuse ; secondly, the comparative method, which was.the
chief conquest of the Restoration and Augustan critics,
but at present is rarely made use of ; thirdly, the his-
toric method introduced for all practical purposes into
English by Dryden, and considerably widened by Car-
lyle ; fourthly, the wsthetic.method, which, with the his-
toric, are the two great schools of criticism at the .pre-
sent day.

THE TRUTH ABOOT THE R.I.C.—IIL

UCCESSIVE governors of Ireland have made occa-
sions to laud the Constabulary, and ¢all them the
finest body of men in the world—a sort of incentive ta
them to exceed their duties when it suited the exigencies
of party politics. Occasionally when under the influence
of strong passion on the part of some, and of prejudice
or bigotry on the part of others, the police exceed all
rational bounds, and interfere in an illegal manner with
the legitimate rights of the people. The Govern-
ment support the police in their improper conduct
towards three-fourths of the people, because it helps to
kill the national spirit to make the people feel that they
have no rights only those the police in their discretion
may allow. The conduct of the police, no matter how
bad, must be justified, or at least passed over, resulting
in many of them considering themselves not the servants,
but the masters of the people.  Good men see the rules
set aside, the toady and the man without principle or
character promoted for menial offices and personal atten-
tion to persons of influence without adequate professional
knowledge while they are ignored, so that you will find a
head constable or a sergeant in charge of one station who
is all that is desirable in courtesy, efficiency and inde-
pendence, and at the next station a man of similar rank
who is an object of pity, but at the same time of fear and
alarm, for he is like a bull in a china shop.
They see the District Inspectors, who do their duties in
a straightforward manner and irrespective of persons,

Joften passed over for promotion, while the man who is

obviously wanting in rectitude, even the man whose low
standard of morality and sobriety have become matters
of public knowledge in some cases, investigated by courts
of law, but, who is machine-like, and ever ready to be
the needful, is advanced.

They have also the extremely bad example set them
of about 150 cadet officers receiving large salaries and
emoluments for doing almost nothing but play lawn
tennis and roam about amongst their friends, scheming
and making excuses for being absent from their duties,
while the same number of head constables perform their
work, the important duties of the latter being conve-
niently overlooked. There are two, and in some cases
three, men employed in each County Inspector’s office
doing one man’s work. They never do more than two
hours’ clerical work, while a civil servant has to give
seven hours’ clerical work.

Hotel, 34 Gardiner’s Place, Dublin. Terms Moderate.

At nearly every headquarters a constable is employed
deing messages and jobs for the officers who receive £45
per annum for a servant. This is really a very expen-
sive system kept on the taxes in this Poor country where
education is starved in the interest of the ascendancy.
R.I.C. cadets come from a class of people who have been
pampered: by: the State as a rule. Their parents have
generally been officials of one kind or another, and they
appear to think they have inherited a right to draw large
salaries for nominal duties. Then, on the other hand
the people are responsible for allowing the :\scendanc)"
to monopolise almost all these casy, well-paid posts.
They ought to send their sons into the police officers,
where they would be able to exercise a healthy influence
on the administration of law and justice in their own
country, and create a greater variety of opinion in the
police. The few Catholics who have control in the
higher 1)1aces are too isolated to do much in this respect,
or to give any considerable support to Irish ideas.
Catholics prefer the second division, the solicitor pro-
fession, which is overcrowded, and even the Civil Ser-
vice in London and India, to well-paid births in their
own country.

In most of the barracks the men and married families
are huddled together in a most improper way. One
weuld think the arrangements were made with a view
to create a minimum possibility of the occupants living
in peace, hence the contention which demands the time
of a superior officer at headquarters to decide. Two
married families often have to cook in the same kitchen.
Sleeping accommodation for married families is not at
all sufficient. It is not unusual for a sergeant, his wife
and four children, sometimes six or seven children, to
sleep in one ,room! No sanitary accommodation for
separate families. A most disgraceful condition of
things in which the common decencies of civilised society
cannot be observed.

Thus economy is practised with a vengeance in the
lower and working grades of the police, while emolu-
ments are showered on the higher and idle ranks. As
regards discipline there is no such thing in the police.
It is enforced slavery under the name of discipline. No
mutual respect or attachment or sympathetic feelings
exist between those who command and those com-
manded. It is sought to compel respect rather than to
win it. There could not be a better illustration of
the words of the great dramatist than that afforded by
the police system :—

‘0, but man, proud man!

Dressed in a little brief authority,

Most ignorant of what he’s most assured. His glassy
essence—like an angry ape,

Plays such fantastic tricks before high heaven

As make the angels weep.”

Discipline is essential, but is best maintained by just
dealing and good example, and should not be invoked
to compel compliance with selfish and personal desires.
The whim and caprice of each person in charge repre-
sents the discipline at that particular post. It is not
recognised that a man has a right to think for himself
and act in obedience to his conscience. A man cannot
tell the truth except at his peril if it involves the man
above him or anyone he is interested in. The writer
entered a house some time ago where there ‘were police
in company of a District Inspector. We were there
about half an hour, and during that time the sergeant
saluted the District Inspector eleven times, L]}at is,
every time he was addressed directly by the District In-
spector. The thing did not secem fulsome to anyone.
The words of the poet must not have appealed to that
District Inspector.

Many matters of importance will be discussed at the
forthcoming Industrial Conference at Limerick.  The
Trish Railways, in their relation to Irish Industries, will
be one of the subjects for discussion. Another matter
on the agenda is—that as a set-off against unfair through
rates on goods coming into Ireland, the various Port
authorities in Ireland be requested to re-arrange their
scale of dues, so as to place the Irish manufacturer on
the same competitive level as the foreigner. The question
of establishing a Department of Industries for Ireland
with adequate funds at its disposal for the development
of industries, ete., is likely to give rise to a lively debate.
The suggestion appears to be that this Department
should be started by Parliament, and Irish members,
irrespective of party, are to be urged to bring the
matter before the Government. Another proposal to be
discussed is the affiliation, under a Central Executive,
of the various lrish Industrial Associations. Amongst
other interesting matters to be discussed are—‘‘That all

A ¢apa,—Somebody has sent me a marked copy of
your issue of November 16th, wherein certain charges
are preferred against the Sinn Fein Organisation in
Clapham by a person named Hennessey.

As the above branch is the only one in the locality, I
hasten to a refutation.

No. 1. The branch has not been recently formed. It
has been working against indifferentism and organised
ignorance for a period of over four years.

No. 2. There is not a volunteer sitting on the com-
mittee, neither is there any such personage a member of
the branch.

Might T suggest to W. B. Hennessey that—notwith-
standing his ambition to appear in print—his time might
be more profitably employed in mastering ‘ O’Growney
I.-V.,” and having accomplished this, set himself to
diffusing the knowledge contained therein, than engag-
ing in the unenviable task of libel-monger. Further-
more, should he possess a hankering after truth it would
be well to first ascertain his grounds, and not rush into
print on the very reliable authority of—Oubaipc bean
